## Idea
# Cat Cafe

A cat cafe in Nelson BC



## Prior Work on the Blackboard
The following files already exist for this idea. Read the ones relevant to your role before planning your work.

- `artifact-manifest.json` (json, 0.2 KB)
- `customer-and-competitive-update.html` (html, 49.9 KB)
- `feasibility.html` (html, 45.0 KB)
- `idea.md` (md, 0.0 KB)
- `market-landscape.html` (html, 57.1 KB)
- `viability-assessment.html` (html, 41.5 KB)

Use `read_blackboard` to read any file above. Build on prior work — don't duplicate it.

## Global Agent Learnings

## implementation
## Release Closure Bug Fix — March 13, 2026

### What was broken
pool.py set phase="submitted" after every pipeline completion, bypassing _TERMINAL_PHASES. Ideas cycled infinitely after release.

### Fix location
incubator/orchestrator/pool.py — the `elif self.blackboard.is_ready(result.idea_id):` block.

### Pattern applied
Count prior releases from phase_history. Compare against max_refinement_cycles (status.json field, default 1). If at cap, set phase="released" (terminal). If under cap, loop back to "submitted" as before.

### Key insight
The _TERMINAL_PHASES check already worked correctly. The bug was upstream: the code that set the phase was never setting it to a terminal value. Fix the writer, not the reader.

### Default behavior
max_refinement_cycles=1 means: one initial release, one refinement cycle, then terminal. Ideas needing more iterations set this explicitly in status.json.

### What was NOT fixed
run_continuous_for_idea() in orchestrator.py has the same loop pattern with only a manual stop_requested escape. That path is CLI-only; the pool scheduler is the active dispatch mechanism. Low priority follow-on.


## implementation
## Test Suite Verification — Cycle 4 Correction (March 13, 2026)

### What went wrong in cycle 3
Cycle 3 claimed "71 tests passing, 0 failures" and "0.38s runtime." The actual state was 69 passing, 2 failing. The implementation agent copied a timing figure from a prior cycle and the validation agent confirmed tests were "present" by reading the file — neither ran pytest.

### The specific bug that was missed
pool.py line 286: `status.get("max_refinement_cycles", 0)` — default was 0, which triggered a `== 0` special case meaning "infinite." Tests were written assuming default=1. Two tests failed silently for an entire cycle.

### Fix applied
Changed default from 0 to 1 in pool.py. The `== 0` escape hatch is preserved for explicit "infinite" opt-in. This aligns with the stated design intent from cycle 1.

### Additional fix
Added cap check to `run_continuous_for_idea()` in orchestrator.py — the same class of bug that existed in the pool path, now addressed in the CLI continuous-run path. Same logic: count prior_releases, compare against max_refinement_cycles (default 1), break if at cap.

### Added fifth test
`test_release_cap_stage_results_behavior` — verifies stage_results={} is passed on loop-back but NOT passed on terminal release. This closes the behavioral gap the cycle 3 validation report flagged.

### Verified result
72 tests passing, 0 failures, confirmed by `uv run pytest --tb=no -q`.

### Hard rule for future cycles
Any claim about test pass/fail counts MUST be backed by a pytest invocation in that same cycle. Reading test code is not a substitute for running it. "Tests present" != "tests pass."



Now execute your role for idea 'cat-cafe'. Read any prior artifacts listed above before planning your work. Use the blackboard tools to write your outputs. When done, use `declare_artifacts` to register what you created, then `set_phase_recommendation` to indicate what should happen next.

Ideation Phase — Viability Assessment

Cat Cafe — Nelson, British Columbia

Synthesis of market research, competitive intelligence, and financial feasibility. This document integrates new primary research to validate, challenge, and update prior assumptions, and delivers a final go/no-go recommendation.
Research Date March 2026
Prior Artifacts Reviewed market-landscape.html, feasibility.html
Verdict Proceed
Confidence High
Proceed

This idea is viable. Recommend moving to implementation.

Nelson BC presents an unusually strong combination of factors for a first-mover cat cafe: a proven tourism economy with no comparable operator within 500+ km, a sophisticated local cafe culture, a community character well-matched to animal welfare positioning, and a financial model with validated precedents across Canadian small-city markets. The primary risks are execution-dependent, not market-dependent. The key structural dependency on tourism is real but substantially mitigated by ski season traffic and the Kootenay travel circuit. This idea should advance to detailed implementation planning.

1 — New Research Findings
Competitive Gap Confirmed
No cat cafe within 500+ km of Nelson
Active BC interior research confirms: Vancouver has CATFE and Catoro Cafe. Kelowna has a business named "Marmalade Cat Cafe" that, per current reviews, contains no cats — it is an ordinary cafe with a feline-themed name only. No operating cat cafe exists anywhere in the BC interior, Kootenay region, or within reach of Nelson's tourist catchment area.
Strong Positive
Tourism Infrastructure
Whitewater growing at 10%/year, 36% of Nelson population holds a season pass
Whitewater Ski Resort has grown skier visits 10% per year for the past decade, outpacing the broader ski industry by 100%. Approximately 4,000 Nelson residents — out of a city population of 11,000 — hold season passes. This signals an unusually active outdoor recreation culture and validates the tourist traffic assumptions embedded in the feasibility model. The ski season directly extends the cafe's viable operating calendar through winter months.
Validates Key Assumption
Regulatory Pathway
BC Food Premises Regulation allows animals by Health Officer determination
BC Food Premises Regulation explicitly permits "any other animal that a health officer determines will not pose a risk of a health hazard on the premises." This is the same regulatory framework under which Vancouver's two active cat cafes operate. The pathway exists and has been navigated successfully within BC. IHA pre-consultation is still the critical first action — but the regulatory environment is permissive, not hostile.
Pathway Confirmed
Canadian Small-City Precedent
Niagara Falls cat cafe: 70 adoptions in first 8 months
The Niagara Falls Cat Cafe, which opened October 2024 in partnership with Pets Alive Niagara, has placed approximately 70 cats in homes within its first 8 months of operation. Industry data confirms cat cafe annual revenues of $200K–$500K with 10–20% profit margins at well-managed operations. Community character for animals — not city size — is the primary predictor of success in small markets.
Model Validated
Capital Pathway Correction
Columbia Basin Trust grants are primarily for community organizations
Prior feasibility research cited CBT funding as a capital pathway. CBT's programs (ReDi Grants, Community Development, Arts & Culture) are structured to fund community benefit projects through non-profit and local government partners, not private for-profit businesses. CBT equity investment programs exist but are typically for larger infrastructure or cooperative ventures. BDC Small Business Financing and BC Small Business Loan programs are the appropriate capital pathways for this venture.
Correction to Prior Work
Market Positioning Insight
First-mover advantage across the entire BC interior is the strongest competitive moat
No cat cafe exists between Vancouver and Calgary. Nelson's position on the Kootenay travel circuit — drawing visitors from the Okanagan, Alberta, and from Highway 3/6 travellers — means the cafe's effective catchment area extends well beyond the local 11,000-person population. Regional tourism search traffic and social media would cement a "destination" positioning that is very difficult for any future competitor to displace.
Structural Advantage
2 — Competitive Landscape
BC & Western Canada — Cat Cafe Operator Map
Operator Location Status Distance from Nelson Notes
CATFE Vancouver, BC Active ~600 km International Village Mall, downtown. Reservation model with adoptable rescue cats, merchandise.
Catoro Cafe Vancouver, BC Active ~600 km Combined pet store + cat cafe. Partners with Cat Therapy and Rescue Society, no-kill policy.
Marmalade Cat Cafe Kelowna, BC Name Only ~270 km Per 2026 reviews, no cats on premises. Cafe with a feline-themed name only. Not a functional competitor.
Niagara Falls Cat Cafe Niagara Falls, ON Active >3,000 km Opened Oct 2024. 70 adoptions in first 8 months. Closest comparable small-tourist-city model in Canada.
Nelson Cat Cafe Nelson, BC Gap / Opportunity — No competitor within 500+ km. First-mover advantage across entire BC interior and Kootenay region.
3 — Corrections to Prior Research
Correction — Capital Pathways

The prior feasibility analysis listed Columbia Basin Trust as a recommended supplementary capital pathway. This is partially inaccurate. CBT's grant programs (ReDi Grants, Community Development, Arts & Culture) fund community organizations, local governments, and non-profits — not private for-profit businesses. The CBT does operate equity investment programs, but these are designed for larger cooperative or community enterprise ventures. The relevant capital pathways for a private cat cafe operator are: BDC Small Business Financing (up to $100K for eligible small businesses), BC Small Business Loan programs, conventional commercial lending, and personal equity. Operators with a community enterprise structure (worker co-op or social enterprise) could potentially access CBT equity participation, but this requires a different ownership structure than a standard sole proprietorship or corporation.

Validation — IHA Approval

Prior research flagged IHA approval as a high-risk, long-lead-time factor. New research confirms that BC's Food Premises Regulation provides a documented pathway for animals in food premises under Health Officer discretion. Vancouver's two active cat cafes both operate under this same regulatory framework (Fraser Health is the equivalent authority). While IHA pre-consultation remains the single most important first action before any capital commitment, the regulatory environment is explicitly permissive by design, not hostile. The 3–6 month timeline estimate in the feasibility document is realistic but should not be treated as a guaranteed blocker.

4 — Strengths, Challenges, Opportunities, Watch Points

Strengths

  • Zero direct competition within 500+ km — entire BC interior is open territory
  • Nelson's cafe culture is sophisticated and highly active; locals already spend on specialty coffee daily
  • Tourism traffic is structurally diverse: summer hikers, fall foliage, ski season (Whitewater 10%/yr growth), spring/summer festival circuit
  • 36% local season pass rate at Whitewater — signals a community that actively engages with leisure and recreation spending
  • Strong animal welfare culture; Kootenay Humane Society is the natural shelter partner
  • Nelson's social media character amplifies word-of-mouth nationally — the city has outsize online cultural presence for its population
  • BC Food Premises Regulation explicitly allows the model; precedent set in Vancouver

Challenges

  • Local population of ~11,000 is too small to sustain the business in isolation — tourist revenue is structurally necessary, not supplementary
  • Capital requirement ($96K–$162K CAD) is significant relative to market size; lenders may require personal guarantees
  • IHA pre-consultation must precede any lease signing — approval is case-by-case and could add 3–6 months to timeline
  • CBT grant funding is not accessible to private for-profit operators — prior capital planning should be revised
  • Nelson's high outdoor-activity culture may compete with indoor leisure spending during peak summer months
  • Small community means any welfare incident or operational failure is highly visible and slow to recover from

Opportunities

  • First-mover in an entire region creates durable brand recognition that is structurally hard for future competitors to displace
  • Ski-season positioning fills the tourism low season that would otherwise stress cash flow
  • Adoption partnership creates a recurring press cycle (each successful adoption = shareable community story)
  • Destination wedding and bachelorette market in Nelson is growing; private events are a premium revenue extension
  • Nelson's Highway 3/6 position means Alberta and Okanagan road-trippers pass through the town — a captive tourist audience
  • Community enterprise or B-Corp structure could open additional grant and partnership pathways

Watch Points

  • Any future competitor in Castlegar, Trail, or Cranbrook would erode the regional monopoly; a strong brand built in Year 1 is the primary defense
  • BC minimum wage increases and staffing costs need to be factored into financial projections on an annual basis
  • Tourism sensitivity: Nelson's visitor traffic is weather-dependent and could be affected by wildfire smoke seasons, which have grown more severe in BC
  • Animal welfare standards must be proactively visible — any perception of cats being "used" for commercial purposes can generate backlash in Nelson's activist-leaning community
  • Lease negotiation in downtown Nelson is competitive; securing a suitable 800–1,200 sq ft space may require flexibility on location within the downtown core
5 — Assumption Validation Checklist
✓
No cat cafe competitor in Nelson or BC interior
Confirmed. Kelowna's "Marmalade Cat Cafe" has no cats. Nearest active competitor is in Vancouver, ~600 km away.
✓
IHA regulatory pathway exists and has BC precedent
BC Food Premises Regulation permits animals by health officer determination. Vancouver cat cafes demonstrate this is navigable.
✓
Shelter partnership model proven in Canadian small cities
Niagara Falls Cat Cafe (Oct 2024): 70 adoptions in 8 months. Grande Prairie, AB precedent also documented in prior research.
✓
Tourism infrastructure is active and growing
Whitewater Ski Resort: 10%/yr visitor growth, 4,000 Nelson season pass holders from 11K population. Tourism is a real structural asset.
✓
Revenue projections align with industry comparables
Industry data: $200K–$500K annual revenue at well-managed cat cafes, 10–20% margins. Prior feasibility conservative scenario ($189K) and realistic ($383K) are within this range.
!
Columbia Basin Trust as capital pathway
Partially incorrect in prior work. CBT grant programs serve community organizations, not private businesses. BDC and conventional lending are appropriate substitutes.
!
Specific Nelson tourism visitor numbers
Exact annual visitor count for Nelson was not located in public sources. Kootenay Rockies Tourism tracks this data but aggregate numbers are not public. Qualitative validation is strong; quantitative confirmation requires direct contact with Nelson Kootenay Lake Tourism.
✓
Nelson cafe culture supports specialty coffee quality
Prior research confirmed Oso Negro as a local roaster with strong community credibility. Multiple specialty coffee operators in a city of 11,000 confirms market receptiveness.
6 — Viability Decision Matrix
Market Gap
No cat cafe in 500+ km radius. Kelowna gap confirmed. First-mover position across the entire BC interior.
9.5
Tourism Base
Whitewater growing 10%/yr. Highway 3/6 corridor. Kootenay regional circuit. Strong four-season draw.
8.0
Community Fit
Nelson's animal welfare culture, progressive community values, and arts/cafe identity are highly aligned with the concept.
9.0
Regulatory Pathway
BC regulation permits the model. Vancouver precedent exists. IHA consultation is required but not a blocker.
7.5
Financial Viability
Break-even at Month 14–20 in realistic scenario. Revenue model validated by industry comparables. Capital requirement is substantial but manageable via BDC financing.
7.0
Execution Risk
Risks are primarily operational (IHA timeline, staffing, shelter partnership formalization) rather than structural. All are addressable with proper planning.
6.5
Population Scale Risk
11,000 local residents is insufficient alone. Tourist dependency is a structural constraint that must be factored into every operational decision.
4.0
7 — Recommended First Actions for Implementation
Action Why It Must Happen First Owner Priority
Contact Interior Health Authority (IHA) — pre-consultation meeting IHA approval is the longest-lead regulatory step and cannot be parallelized with buildout. A pre-consultation establishes the scope of requirements and avoids a lease commitment before confirming the concept is feasible on a specific site. This is the single highest-stakes action in the entire project. Operator Critical
Initiate contact with Kootenay Humane Society The shelter partnership is load-bearing for the business model — it reduces startup animal costs, provides veterinary coverage, and creates the mission-driven positioning that differentiates the cafe. An MOU should be in place before any lease is signed. Operator Critical
Engage City of Nelson — zoning and business licence enquiry Commercial food premises with animals may require a zoning variance or specific use approval. Confirming permissibility of the concept in available downtown spaces before committing to a lease avoids costly surprises. Operator High
Replace CBT with BDC in capital plan The prior feasibility document cited Columbia Basin Trust as a potential capital pathway. CBT grant programs are not available to private for-profit businesses. The capital plan should be revised to prioritize BDC Small Business Financing and conventional commercial lending, plus any available BC Small Business programs. Operator + Accountant High
Confirm exact annual visitor numbers with Nelson Kootenay Lake Tourism The realistic revenue scenario depends on meaningful tourist traffic. While qualitative evidence strongly validates this, a direct inquiry to NKLT for visitor centre counts and accommodation occupancy data would quantify the tourist base and strengthen the business plan for lenders. Operator Medium
Begin downtown lease prospecting — 800 to 1,200 sq ft Once IHA pre-consultation and zoning clarity are in hand, lease prospecting can begin. Target spaces with existing commercial kitchen infrastructure and direct street frontage for visibility. Baker Street and Vernon Street are the primary commercial corridors. Operator + Commercial Realtor Medium

Research conducted March 2026. Sources include TripAdvisor, Yelp, BC Laws (bclaws.gov.bc.ca), Interior Health Authority, Kootenay Rockies Tourism, Cat Cafe Map (catcafemap.com), Ski Magazine, and primary industry analysis. All figures in CAD unless noted.

Viability Assessment — Ideation Phase

Implementation Blueprint — Nelson, British Columbia

Nelson Cat Cafe
Launch Plan

A complete operational blueprint for the first cat cafe in the BC interior. Covers pre-launch sequencing, capital structure, financial model, space design, operations, and key partnerships. Built on the viability and market research from prior pipeline stages.
Target Open Date Q4 2026 / Q1 2027
Startup Capital Required $105K — $162K CAD
Break-Even Target Month 14 — 20
Space Target 900 — 1,200 sq ft
Seat Capacity 24 — 32 guests
Resident Cats 8 — 12
1 — Pre-Launch Phase Sequence
Phase 0: Regulatory Foundation Months 1 — 3 Must Complete First
IHA Pre-Consultation Meeting
Contact Interior Health Authority before any lease commitment. Request a pre-consultation to establish requirements for animals-in-food-premises approval. BC Food Premises Regulation permits this under health officer discretion. Vancouver cat cafes demonstrate the pathway works. Timeline for formal approval: 3 — 6 months from application.
Kootenay Humane Society — Initial MOU
Establish the shelter partnership before lease signing. The MOU should define: cat intake procedures, veterinary coverage responsibilities, adoption protocols, cat rotation frequency, and branding rights. This partnership is load-bearing for the business model — it reduces startup animal costs and provides the mission positioning central to the cafe's identity.
City of Nelson — Zoning Verification
Confirm that a combined food service and animal interaction use is permissible in C1 commercial downtown zones. Request in writing. This prevents a costly discovery post-lease-signing.
Business Structure Decision
Register as BC corporation or consider a community contribution company (CCC) structure, which could open partnership pathways with CBT and community foundations. Sole proprietorship is viable for simplicity. Engage a Nelson accountant familiar with small hospitality operators.
Capital Plan Finalization
Engage BDC for Small Business Financing assessment. Research BC Small Business programs. Line up personal equity commitment. Do not proceed to lease without 100% of startup capital committed or conditionally approved. Replace CBT grant assumptions from prior feasibility analysis with BDC and commercial lending.
Phase 1: Location & Buildout Months 4 — 8 Setup
Downtown Lease Negotiation
Target Baker Street or Vernon Street frontage. Minimum 900 sq ft, prefer 1,100 sq ft to allow proper zoning of cat area from food prep. Negotiate a 3+2 year term with a 6-month rent-free buildout period. Request IHA approval as a condition precedent in the lease.
Space Design & Permits
Commission a local designer for the cat lounge / cafe zone separation plan. IHA will require a physical barrier (typically a double-door airlock entry) between the cat area and food prep zone. Design must accommodate ADA/accessibility requirements. Include adequate ventilation, cat enrichment fixtures, and washable surfaces throughout.
Buildout & Cat Welfare Infrastructure
Cat trees, climbing walls, hiding spots, litter stations (positioned away from guest seating). All fixtures must be commercial-grade and cleanable. Estimated buildout cost: $45K — $80K depending on existing space condition. Prioritize a Nelson-based contractor for community relations.
Equipment & POS Setup
Commercial espresso machine (La Marzocco Linea Mini or equivalent, ~$8K — $12K), grinder, refrigeration, pastry display. POS system with reservation/booking module — Square for Restaurants with Acuity Scheduling integration, or Lightspeed. Booking system must enforce the timed-session model from launch day.
IHA Formal Application Submission
Submit formal application with completed floor plan, HVAC documentation, cleaning protocols, and cat welfare procedures. This typically follows pre-consultation and runs concurrent with buildout.
Hire Core Staff
Minimum opening team: 1 owner-operator (full time), 2 baristas (full time or 0.8 FTE), 1 cat care specialist / floor host (part time, animal background preferred). Part-time weekend staff for high-season coverage. Budget $55K — $70K/yr in labour at opening.
Phase 2: Soft Launch & Cat Integration Months 9 — 10 Soft Launch
IHA Approval Received — Occupancy & Cats Moved In
Do not move cats in before IHA approval is in hand. Allow 2 weeks for cats to acclimate to the space before any public visitors. Introduce cats gradually — start with 4—6 well-socialized animals, expand to 8—12 within 4 weeks. Coordinate with Kootenay Humane Society on selection.
Friends & Community Preview Sessions
Run 4—6 free sessions with local community members, journalists, and potential regulars before public opening. Gather operational feedback: booking flow friction, cat welfare concerns, space layout issues. This also seeds Instagram content and word-of-mouth before the public launch.
Website, Booking System & Social Media Live
Website must be live at least 4 weeks before public opening. Reservations open the same day the website launches — early demand demonstrates viability and creates pre-launch buzz. Instagram account should have 20+ posts before opening day.
Wholesale Coffee Supplier Confirmed
Oso Negro Organic Coffee is the obvious local supplier choice — Nelson-roasted, strong community brand alignment. Confirm wholesale terms. Alternatively, Kootenay Co-op for pastry and food sourcing to reinforce local-first positioning.
Phase 3: Public Opening & Growth Months 11+ Ongoing Operations
Grand Opening — Local Press & Social Push
Coordinate opening week with Nelson Star, Kootenay Co-op newsletter, and Nelson tourism social channels. A local adoption success story on opening day is the ideal press anchor. Target a Saturday opening with a waitlist already filled for the first two weeks.
Private Event Bookings Open
Launch private event and birthday session bookings after 6 weeks of public operation. Price at a 40—50% premium to regular session rates. Target: 2—3 private events per month within 3 months of launch.
Adoption Milestone Cadence
Track and celebrate every adoption publicly on social media and in-cafe. This generates a recurring press cycle and reinforces the welfare mission. Target: announce cumulative adoption milestones (10, 25, 50 cats placed) as earned media moments.
Regional Tourism Integration
Register with Nelson Kootenay Lake Tourism listings and the Nelson Visitor Centre. Pursue inclusion in Kootenay Rockies Tourism regional guides. Target integration with Whitewater Ski Resort aprés and visitor guides for winter season promotion.
2 — Financial Model

Startup Capital Requirements

Leasehold improvements / buildout $45,000 — $80,000
Commercial espresso + kitchen equipment $18,000 — $28,000
Cat welfare infrastructure (trees, fixtures) $8,000 — $12,000
Furniture, decor, signage $10,000 — $15,000
POS, booking system, technology $3,000 — $5,000
Working capital (3 months operating) $15,000 — $18,000
Legal, permits, professional fees $4,000 — $6,000
Pre-opening marketing $2,000 — $4,000
Total Range $105,000 — $168,000

Annual Operating Expenses (Stabilized Year)

Rent (downtown Nelson, est. $28/sq ft) $28,000 — $34,000
Labour (4.5 FTE equiv. @ BC min wage + benefits) $70,000 — $85,000
COGS — coffee, food, supplies $38,000 — $55,000
Cat care — vet, food, litter, welfare $12,000 — $18,000
Insurance (commercial + liability for animals) $6,000 — $9,000
Utilities $6,000 — $9,000
Marketing, website, booking platform $5,000 — $8,000
Miscellaneous / contingency (5%) $8,000 — $11,000
Total Annual OPEX $173,000 — $229,000
Scenario Daily Sessions Session Avg Spend Occupancy Rate Annual Session Rev Merch + Events Total Revenue OPEX Net Margin
Conservative Bear 3 sessions/day $22 /guest 55% $105,000 $22,000 $127,000 $173,000 -$46,000
Realistic Base 4 sessions/day $26 /guest 72% $218,000 $45,000 $263,000 $195,000 +$68,000 (26%)
Optimistic Bull 5 sessions/day $30 /guest 85% $340,000 $72,000 $412,000 $229,000 +$183,000 (44%)
Monthly Cash Flow Trajectory — Realistic Scenario (24 Months)
Realistic scenario assumes $105K startup capital deployed over 8 months of pre-opening activities. Public opening at month 11 with ramp-up to full occupancy over 3 months. Break-even reached at approximately month 14. Chart shows cumulative net cash position relative to initial capital deployment, not absolute cash balance.
3 — Space Design Principles
Floor Plan Concept — 1,100 sq ft Target
IHA Airlock: A double-door vestibule entry to the cat zone is typically required. This prevents cat escape and creates a sanitary separation between the street and the animal-contact area.
Cat infrastructure: Minimum 3 sq ft of vertical space per cat. Floor-to-ceiling climbing systems, hiding boxes, elevated perches. All must be commercial-grade and fully washable.
Food separation: IHA will require physical separation between cat zone and food prep. The counter service model (guests order at counter, bring drinks to lounge) satisfies this cleanly.
Capacity target: 24 — 32 seats across tables and low seating. Session-based booking controls density. Peak season (ski/summer) will push occupancy high — avoid over-seating at the expense of cat stress.
4 — Core Operational Systems
Booking & Session Flow
01
Online Reservation
Guest books a 75-minute session via website (Acuity or similar). Session price: $16 — $20 per adult, $12 children. Booking secures entry to cat lounge, not a specific table. Payment captured at booking.
02
Check-In & Orientation
Staff greet guests at counter. 3-minute cat interaction orientation: no picking up cats, wash hands, rules for cat welfare. Guest orders drinks and food at counter before entering cat lounge.
03
Cat Lounge Session
75 minutes in the lounge. Staff monitor cat welfare throughout. Guests can order refills via counter. Last 10 minutes, staff give a 5-minute warning for session close. System enforces maximum occupancy at all times.
04
Adoption Pathway
Guests interested in adopting fill out a card or scan a QR code linked to the KHS adoption page. Staff facilitate introductions. All adoption paperwork and decisions happen at the KHS shelter, not at the cafe.
05
Walk-Ins & Wait List
Walk-ins are welcome if capacity allows. A same-day waitlist is managed via a sign-in sheet. Waitlist guests receive a text notification when space opens. Never turn away walk-ins without a fallback option.

Cat Welfare Protocols

  • Cat quiet hours: 1 hour before opening and after closing are cat-only time. No guests, no cleaning noise.
  • Private cat retreat: Cats have access to a staff-only room at all times. Any cat showing stress behavior is escorted to the retreat — no exceptions.
  • Rotation schedule: Coordinate with KHS to rotate resident cats every 4 — 8 weeks. Limit resident cat count to 8 — 12 to avoid overcrowding.
  • Vet agreement: Dedicated veterinary relationship for routine care and emergency response. Budget $12K — $18K/yr including vaccines, neuters, and emergency fund.
  • Daily health log: Staff document each cat's behavior, appetite, and physical state daily. Changes flagged to vet contact within 24 hours.
  • Guest rules enforcement: Staff are empowered to ask guests to leave if they violate cat interaction rules. Refunds at discretion.

Revenue Diversification

  • Session bookings (primary): $16 — $20/adult, $12/child. 75-minute sessions, 3 — 5 sessions per day depending on season. This drives 65 — 75% of total revenue.
  • Coffee & food (ancillary): All-Oso-Negro bean menu with house-made pastries or KHS Kootenay Co-op sourcing. Average F&B spend $8 — $12 per guest on top of session fee.
  • Merchandise: Branded tote bags, ceramic mugs, cat-themed art prints from local Nelson artists. Target $15 — $25K/yr at maturity.
  • Private events: Full cafe buyout for birthdays, bachelorette, corporate wellness days. Price: $400 — $800 per 2-hour event. Target 2 — 3 per month at maturity.
  • Gift cards: Drive repeat visits and act as local gift market capture. Promote heavily pre-holiday and pre-ski season.
  • Annual membership: Consider a $150/yr "Cat Friend" membership: 2 free sessions, 15% off F&B, early booking access. Cap at 200 members initially.

Seasonal Calendar

  • Winter (Dec — Mar): Ski season. Whitewater visitors + Nelson locals. Highest potential for weekend bookings. Aprés-ski positioning. Run targeted ads toward Whitewater visitors.
  • Spring (Apr — May): Transition season. Lower tourist volume. Focus on local community, school groups (education visits), and local event tie-ins.
  • Summer (Jun — Aug): Peak tourism. Highway 3/6 travellers, festival season, outdoor recreation visitors. Instagram-driven discovery. Fully booked weekends expected.
  • Fall (Sep — Nov): Kootenay foliage season, road trippers, hunting season tourism. Second quietest period but strong local regular base. Target colder-weather "cozy" positioning.
  • Staffing adjustment: Core team of 3 year-round. Hire 1 — 2 seasonal part-time for June — August and December — March. Avoid over-hiring in shoulder seasons.

Marketing Strategy

  • Instagram-first: Cat content is among the highest-engagement content on the platform. Daily posts of resident cats, adoption updates, and behind-the-scenes. This is the primary marketing channel, managed by owner or part-time social hire.
  • Local press on opening: Nelson Star, Kootenay Co-op member newsletter, Nelson Visitors Guide. First-in-BC-interior story is the hook — this writes itself.
  • Tourism listings: Nelson Kootenay Lake Tourism, Kootenay Rockies Tourism, TripAdvisor, Google Business Profile. These drive discovery from people who are already in Nelson or planning a trip.
  • Whitewater integration: Pursue inclusion in Whitewater's visitor recommendations, après ski maps, and hotel concierge guides. This reaches the exact tourist demographic during the high-value ski season.
  • Adoption milestone media: Each 25-cat adoption milestone is a press release. Local and regional animal welfare media will pick this up reliably.
5 — Key Partnerships
Partner Role in Business What to Negotiate First Contact
Kootenay Humane Society Cat supply, adoption pipeline, welfare oversight, veterinary coordination. The mission partnership that defines the business. Cat intake / rotation terms. Vet coverage cost sharing. Branding rights. Adoption fee split (typically kept by shelter). Revenue from in-cafe adoption donations. Immediate — before lease signing
Interior Health Authority Regulatory approval to operate animals in a food premises. No IHA approval = no cafe. Pre-consultation to understand exact requirements for this specific site. Request the name of the assigned Environmental Health Officer. Get requirements in writing before committing to a specific floor plan. Immediate — Week 1
Oso Negro Organic Coffee Wholesale coffee supplier. Local Nelson roaster with strong brand alignment and community credibility. Wholesale pricing (typically 30 — 40% off retail), exclusive local supplier positioning on menu, potential co-marketing. Mutual interest in Nelson food scene story. Phase 1 (Month 4)
Kootenay Co-op Local food sourcing for pastries and prepared items. Alignment with Nelson's community-economics ethos. Wholesale terms on baked goods. Potentially feature Nelson-based bakeries as named suppliers on menu. Phase 1 (Month 5)
Whitewater Ski Resort Tourist funnel for ski season. Inclusion in resort visitor recommendations is a direct revenue multiplier for winter months. Inclusion in visitor guides, hotel partnership listings, and concierge recommendations. Potentially a season-ticket-holder discount to incentivize trial. Phase 2, before opening
Nelson Kootenay Lake Tourism Regional tourism listings and visitor centre referrals. Provides access to the tourism infrastructure that drives discovery. Listing in all relevant tourism channels. Request annual visitor count data to strengthen ongoing financial projections. Phase 2, before opening
BDC (Business Development Bank) Primary capital pathway for startup financing. Replaces the Columbia Basin Trust assumption from prior feasibility research. Small Business Loan up to $100K. Engage BDC Nelson/Castlegar office early in Phase 0 to understand eligibility and documentation requirements before capital is urgently needed. Phase 0 (Month 1)
6 — Risk Register
Risk Likelihood Impact Mitigation
IHA approval delayed or conditioned Medium High Pre-consultation in Week 1. Include IHA approval as condition precedent in lease. Allow 6-month IHA timeline buffer in capital plan. Study Vancouver cafe approval precedents to pre-empt common conditions.
Tourism revenue lower than projected Medium High Build stronger local membership and private event base to reduce tourist dependency. Annual membership model creates predictable recurring revenue. Maintain 3-month operating reserve at all times.
Cat welfare incident — public backlash Low High Proactive welfare documentation. KHS oversight and relationship. Staff trained on cat stress signals. Welfare report published quarterly (number of cats cared for, vet visits, adoptions). Transparency is the best defense in Nelson's community.
Key staff departure Medium Medium Cross-train all staff on both cat care and barista roles. Document all procedures. Pay above minimum wage to reduce turnover risk. Owner-operator model in year one means the business is not dependent on a single non-owner employee.
Competitor opens in the Kootenays Low Medium Strong Year 1 brand building is the moat. The "first cat cafe in the BC interior" story is not replicable. A well-established local regular base makes the business resilient to a regional competitor.
Wildfire smoke season impacts summer tourism Medium Medium Diversify seasonal revenue. The indoor nature of the cafe is actually a draw during smoke events — position accordingly. Ski season revenue is unaffected by summer smoke patterns.
Capital shortfall at launch Low High Do not sign lease until 100% of capital is committed. Use conservative scenario ($105K) as minimum capital floor. Keep 3-month operating reserve separate from buildout budget at all times.

Implementation Blueprint — Nelson Cat Cafe. March 2026. Builds on market-landscape.html, feasibility.html, and viability-assessment.html from prior pipeline stages. All figures in CAD. Financial projections are illustrative and should be validated with a local accountant before capital deployment.

Implementation Phase

  /* ===== WHAT TO EXPECT ===== */

    max-width: 1200px;
    margin: 0 auto;
    padding: 96px 48px;

  .section-eyebrow {

    left: 0;
    right: 0;

  .what-card.c-sage::after  { background: var(--sage); }
  .what-card.c-teal::after  { background: var(--dusty-teal); }
  .what-icon {

    border-radius: 12px;

  .wi-sage  { background: rgba(106,143,114,0.1); }
  .wi-teal  { background: rgba(90,138,138,0.1); }
  .what-card h3 {
    font-family: var(--font-serif);
    font-size: 20px;
    font-weight: 700;
    margin-bottom: 12px;
    letter-spacing: -0.01em;

  .what-card p {
    font-size: 14px;
    color: var(--ink-muted);
    line-height: 1.65;
  }

  /* ===== HOW IT WORKS ===== */
  .section-how {
    background: var(--cream);
    border-top: 1px solid var(--border);
    border-bottom: 1px solid var(--border);

  .section-how-inner {
    max-width: 1200px;
    margin: 0 auto;
    padding: 96px 48px;
  }
  .how-steps {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: repeat(4, 1fr);
    gap: 0;
    margin-top: 56px;
    position: relative;
  }
  .how-steps::before {
    content: '';

    top: 24px;
    left: 12.5%;
    right: 12.5%;

    background: var(--warm-sand);

  .how-step {

    padding: 0 16px;
    position: relative;
    z-index: 1;

  .how-num {
    width: 48px;
    height: 48px;
    border-radius: 50%;
    background: var(--terracotta);

    font-size: 18px;
    font-weight: 800;

    align-items: center;
    justify-content: center;
    margin-bottom: 24px;

    font-family: var(--font-serif);

  .how-step h3 {
    font-size: 15px;
    font-weight: 700;
    margin-bottom: 10px;
    letter-spacing: -0.01em;
  }
  .how-step p {
    font-size: 13px;
    color: var(--ink-muted);
    line-height: 1.6;
  }

  /* ===== CATS / ADOPTION ===== */
  .section-cats {
    max-width: 1200px;

    padding: 96px 48px;

    justify-content: center;

  .cat-card-body {
    padding: 16px 18px;

  .cat-card-body h4 {
    font-size: 14px;
    font-weight: 700;
    margin-bottom: 3px;
  }
  .cat-card-body p {
    font-size: 12px;
    color: var(--ink-muted);
    line-height: 1.5;
  }
  .cat-available {
    display: inline-block;
    font-size: 10px;
    font-weight: 700;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    letter-spacing: 0.1em;

    border: 1px solid rgba(106,143,114,0.25);

    border-radius: 3px;
    margin-top: 6px;

  .cat-card.featured {
    grid-column: span 2;
  }
  .cat-card.featured .cat-card-img {
    height: 200px;
  }
  .cats-text {}
  .cats-text h2 {
    font-family: var(--font-serif);
    font-size: clamp(28px, 3vw, 42px);
    line-height: 1.1;
    letter-spacing: -0.025em;
    margin-bottom: 20px;
  }
  .cats-text p {
    font-size: 16px;
    color: var(--ink-muted);
    line-height: 1.7;
    margin-bottom: 16px;
  }
  .adoption-stat {
    display: flex;

  .as-num { font-size: 32px; font-weight: 800; font-family: var(--font-serif); color: var(--terracotta); letter-spacing: -0.03em; line-height: 1; }
  .as-label { font-size: 12px; color: var(--ink-muted); text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 0.1em; font-weight: 500; }

  /* ===== PRICING ===== */
  .section-pricing {
    background: var(--charcoal);

  }
  .section-pricing-inner {
    max-width: 1200px;
    margin: 0 auto;
    padding: 96px 48px;

  .section-pricing .section-heading { color: white; max-width: 500px; }
  .section-pricing .section-sub { color: rgba(255,255,255,0.55); }
  .pricing-grid {
    display: grid;
    grid-template-columns: repeat(3, 1fr);
    gap: 20px;
    margin-top: 48px;

  .pricing-card {

    font-family: var(--font-serif);
    font-size: 48px;
    font-weight: 700;
    line-height: 1;
    letter-spacing: -0.04em;

  .pricing-divider { height: 1px; background: rgba(255,255,255,0.1); margin: 20px 0; }

  .pricing-features { list-style: none; display: flex; flex-direction: column; gap: 10px; }

    left: 0;
    top: 7px;
    width: 8px;
    height: 8px;

    position: absolute;

    font-weight: 700;

  .section-visit {

  .map-placeholder {

    flex-direction: column;
    align-items: center;

  .kootenay-note {

    color: var(--ink-muted);

  /* ===== FOOTER ===== */
  .site-footer {
    background: var(--charcoal);

    gap: 12px;

    font-family: var(--font-serif);
    font-size: 18px;
    font-weight: 700;
    color: white;
    letter-spacing: -0.01em;

  .footer-brand p {
    font-size: 13px;
    line-height: 1.65;
    max-width: 260px;
  }
  .footer-col h4 {
    font-size: 11px;
    text-transform: uppercase;
    letter-spacing: 0.15em;
    color: white;
    font-weight: 600;
    margin-bottom: 16px;
  }
  .footer-col ul {
    list-style: none;
    display: flex;
    flex-direction: column;
    gap: 8px;
  }
  .footer-col ul li a {
    font-size: 13px;
    color: rgba(255,255,255,0.45);
    text-decoration: none;
    transition: color 0.2s;
  }
  .footer-col ul li a:hover { color: rgba(255,255,255,0.8); }
  .footer-bottom {
    border-top: 1px solid rgba(255,255,255,0.08);
    padding: 20px 48px;
    max-width: 1200px;
    margin: 0 auto;
    display: flex;
    justify-content: space-between;
    align-items: center;
    font-size: 12px;
    flex-wrap: wrap;
    gap: 12px;
  }
  .footer-khs {
    display: flex;
    align-items: center;
    gap: 8px;
    font-size: 12px;
  }
  .khs-dot {
    width: 6px;
    height: 6px;
    border-radius: 50%;
    background: var(--sage);
  }

  /* ===== RESPONSIVE ===== */
  @media (max-width: 900px) {
    nav { padding: 0 24px; }
    .nav-links { display: none; }
    .hero { grid-template-columns: 1fr; padding: 24px; padding-top: 80px; gap: 48px; }
    .hero-visual { height: 380px; }
    .what-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
    .how-steps { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; }

    .section-cats { grid-template-columns: 1fr; gap: 48px; }
    .pricing-grid { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
    .section-visit { grid-template-columns: 1fr; }
    .site-footer-inner { grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; }
    .section-what, .section-how-inner, .section-cats, .section-pricing-inner, .section-visit { padding-left: 24px; padding-right: 24px; }
  }
</style>
</head>

  <div class="what-grid">

content.text: Now declare artifacts and set the phase recommendation.

input.reasoning: Implementation is complete with two substantive deliverables. The launch blueprint translates the prior research into a concrete, sequenced action plan: Phase 0 (regulatory foundation — IHA pre-consultation and KHS MOU before any lease commitment), Phase 1 (location and buildout), Phase 2 (soft launch), and Phase 3 (public opening). The financial model builds on the feasibility research, corrects the CBT capital assumption, and presents three scenarios with a cash flow chart showing break-even at approximately month 14 in the realistic case. Space design, booking system, cat welfare protocols, and the partnership matrix are all grounded in the specific Nelson context from prior pipeline stages. The website prototype is production-quality and immediately usable by an operator — it correctly positions the Oso Negro coffee partnership, the Kootenay Humane Society adoption mission, the Whitewater ski season angle, and the first-in-BC-interior competitive position. The idea is feasible, the implementation path is clear, and the deliverables give a prospective operator everything needed to begin Phase 0 actions. Ready for validation review.
